slides.text: Play 'I Spy' by saying 'I spy with my little eye something that starts with /m/' and help child identify the correct object.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now for our fun game! When I say 'I spy something that starts with /m/', you get to guess which thing it is. Listen carefully to that first sound!

slides.prompt: Take turns playing I Spy, letting the child give clues too. Focus on identifying /m/ sounds versus other beginning sounds.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now you can be the spy too! Pick something that starts with /m/ and give me a clue. I love playing this listening game with you!

items: Can the child make the /m/ sound clearly?
Do they identify at least 2 objects that start with /m/?
Can they distinguish between /m/ and non-/m/ beginning sounds?
slides.mascot_explanation: You're such a good listener! Can you show me how to make the /m/ sound? And can you find the things that start with that sound? You're learning so much!

slides.text: Review all the objects that start with /m/ and celebrate the child's listening skills. Practice the /m/ sound one more time together.
slides.mastery_check: Child can identify /m/ sound and distinguish it from other beginning sounds in at least 2-3 objects.
slides.mascot_explanation: What a wonderful job listening for the /m/ sound! Let's say all our /m/ words one more time: mirror, mug, mittens. You have such good ears for sounds!

title: Number Rods 1-3: One-to-One Counting with Finger Touching
description: Children practice counting quantities 1, 2, and 3 using rods or sticks while touching each unit. This concrete counting experience builds understanding of quantity and number sequence.
instructions: Prepare 3 different length rods using wooden sticks, straws, or blocks (1 unit, 2 units, 3 units)
Start with just the rod of 1, having child touch it while saying 'one'
Introduce the rod of 2, showing how to touch each section while counting 'one, two'
Add the rod of 3, demonstrating touching each unit while counting 'one, two, three'
Let child practice touching and counting each rod multiple times
Mix up the rods and have child arrange them from shortest to longest
Practice counting each rod again while touching the units
End by having child count all three rods in sequence
parent_notes: The key is one-to-one correspondence - each touch represents one unit. At this age, children need concrete, tactile counting experiences. Don't rush to abstract numbers yet. The physical manipulation helps build neural pathways for mathematical thinking. Expect repetition and go slowly.

slides.heading: Number Rods 1-3
slides.mascot_explanation: Hi friends! I'm Betsy Bear and today we're going to count with our special rods! We'll use our fingers to touch and count - it's going to be so much fun!

slides.setup_instructions: Create three rods of different lengths: one unit long, two units long, and three units long. Make sure the units are clearly defined so child can touch each one.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at our special counting rods! I made them different sizes - one short, one medium, and one longer. Each one helps us practice counting in a special way!

slides.text: Start with the shortest rod (1 unit). Have the child touch it with their finger while saying 'one'.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's start with our shortest rod. Put your finger right on it and say 'one' - that's it! This rod shows us what 'one' feels like and looks like.

slides.text: Show the rod of 2 units. Demonstrate touching each section separately while counting 'one, two'.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now here's our medium rod! See how it has two parts? Touch the first part and say 'one', then touch the second part and say 'two'. Each touch is one number!

slides.prompt: Let child practice touching and counting each rod multiple times, then arrange them from shortest to longest.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now it's your turn to touch and count! Take your time with each rod - touch each part as you count. Then see if you can put them in order from smallest to biggest!

items: Does the child touch each unit while counting?
Can they count 1, 2, 3 in the correct sequence?
Do they arrange the rods from shortest to longest correctly?
slides.mascot_explanation: You're doing such a great job counting! Show me how you touch each part while you count. Can you put the rods in order too? I'm so proud of your careful counting!

slides.text: Practice counting all three rods in sequence one final time, emphasizing the one-to-one correspondence of touching and counting.
slides.mastery_check: Child consistently touches each unit while counting and can sequence rods 1-3 from shortest to longest.
slides.mascot_explanation: What wonderful counting! Let's count all our rods one more time together - one, one-two, one-two-three. You're becoming such a good counter with your careful finger touching!

title: Finger Painting Exploration: Primary Color Mixing with Red and Blue
description: Children explore finger painting techniques using red and blue paint, discovering how colors mix when combined. This sensory art experience develops creativity and color awareness.
instructions: Set up a protected work space with newspaper or plastic covering
Provide red and blue washable finger paints and large paper
Start by letting child explore each color separately on paper
Demonstrate how to use different fingers and hand movements
Show what happens when red and blue paints touch each other
Encourage experimentation with mixing the colors
Let child create freely, focusing on the process not the product
Clean up together and display the artwork proudly
materials_needed: Red and blue washable finger paints
parent_notes: Focus on the sensory experience and process, not creating a perfect product. This age learns through exploration and experimentation. The mess is part of the learning! Color mixing at this stage is discovery-based, not formal color theory. Follow the child's interests and natural curiosity.

slides.heading: Finger Painting Exploration
slides.subheading: Primary Color Mixing with Red and Blue
slides.mascot_explanation: Hello artists! I'm Betsy Bear and today we're going to paint with our fingers! We have beautiful red and blue paint to explore and see what happens when they meet!

items: Red and blue washable finger paints
slides.setup_instructions: Cover the work area completely and have child wear old clothes or smock. Put paints in small containers and have cleaning supplies ready nearby.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's get ready for our painting adventure! We need to cover everything up because finger painting can be wonderfully messy. That's part of the fun!

slides.text: Let the child explore red paint first, showing different ways to use fingers, palms, and hands on the paper.
slides.mascot_explanation: Start with our beautiful red paint! You can use just one finger, or your whole hand, or even make dots and lines. There's no wrong way - just explore and have fun!

slides.text: Introduce blue paint on a different area of paper, then show what happens when the two colors meet and blend.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now let's try our lovely blue paint! Put it somewhere new on your paper, and watch what amazing thing happens when red and blue touch each other!

slides.prompt: Allow free exploration and experimentation with color mixing, encouraging the child to discover purple through play.
slides.mascot_explanation: This is your time to be a color scientist! Mix and blend and see what new colors you can make. I wonder what will happen when you swirl red and blue together?

items: Is the child engaged in the sensory experience?
Do they notice when red and blue create purple?
Are they experimenting with different finger movements and techniques?
slides.mascot_explanation: You're such a wonderful artist! Tell me what you discovered - what happens when red and blue come together? I love seeing all the different ways you're using your fingers!

slides.text: Clean hands and workspace together, then admire the artwork and talk about the colors discovered during painting.
slides.mastery_check: Child engages in sensory exploration and shows awareness of color changes when red and blue mix.
slides.mascot_explanation: What beautiful art you created! Look at all the wonderful colors you discovered. Let's clean up together and then we can admire your masterpiece!

title: Goodnight Moon: Fourth Reading with Moon and Stars Discussion
description: Fourth reading of Goodnight Moon with focus on the moon and stars in the story. Children engage with familiar text while exploring nighttime sky concepts and bedtime routines.
instructions: Settle into a cozy reading spot with good lighting
Show the book cover and remind child this is a favorite we've read before
Read slowly, allowing child to anticipate familiar phrases
Point out the moon and stars throughout the illustrations
Pause to discuss how the room gets darker as night progresses
Encourage child to say familiar lines along with you
After reading, talk about nighttime routines and what helps us sleep
End with a quiet moment looking at the final 'goodnight' page
parent_notes: Repetition is crucial at this age for language development and comprehension. Children love predicting what comes next in familiar stories. Use this fourth reading to go deeper into details and make connections to the child's own bedtime routine. The rhythm and rhyme support phonological awareness.

slides.heading: Goodnight Moon
slides.subheading: Fourth Reading with Moon and Stars Discussion
slides.mascot_explanation: Hello sleepy friends! I'm Betsy Bear and we're going to read one of our very favorite books again - Goodnight Moon! I love this cozy bedtime story so much, and I think you do too!

slides.setup_instructions: Create a cozy reading environment with soft lighting. Have the child settle in comfortably where they can see the book clearly.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's make our reading time extra cozy! Get comfortable and maybe grab a soft blanket. We want to feel just like the little bunny getting ready for bed in our story!

slides.text: Show the cover and discuss how this is our fourth time reading this favorite book together.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at our special book! We've read this together three times already, so you probably know what's going to happen. That makes it even more fun to read!

slides.text: Read slowly and point out the moon and stars in each illustration, discussing how they appear throughout the story.
slides.mascot_explanation: As we read, let's look for the moon and stars on every page! See how they're always watching over the little bunny as he gets ready for bed? How special!

slides.prompt: Read the complete book, encouraging child participation and discussion about how the room changes from light to dark.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now let's read our story together! You can say the parts you remember along with me. Notice how the room gets darker and darker as bedtime gets closer!

items: Does the child anticipate familiar phrases?
Can they identify the moon and stars in the illustrations?
Do they notice how the room gets progressively darker?
slides.mascot_explanation: You know this story so well! Can you tell me about the moon and stars you saw? And did you notice how the room changed from light to dark? You're such a good reader!

slides.text: Discuss bedtime routines and what helps us feel ready for sleep, connecting to the bunny's routine in the story.
slides.mastery_check: Child shows familiarity with story sequence and can discuss elements like the moon, stars, and changing light in the room.
slides.mascot_explanation: What a peaceful story! Just like the little bunny, we all have special things that help us get ready for bed. What helps you feel cozy and ready for sweet dreams?
